papers.title: Constraining the initial conditions of NGC 2264 using ejected stars found in Gaia DR2
papers.author_names: Christina Schoettler Richard J. Parker Jos de Bruijne

abstract.text: Fast, ejected stars have been found around several young star-forming regions, such as the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC). These ejected stars can be used to constrain the initial density, spatial and kinematic substructure when compared to predictions from $N$-body simulations. We search for runaway and slower walkaway stars using $Gaia$ DR2 within 100 pc of NGC 2264, which contains subclustered regions around higher-mass OB-stars (S Mon, IRS 1 and IRS 2). We find five runaways and nine walkaways that trace back to S Mon and six runaways and five walkaways that trace back to IRS 1/2 based on their 3D-kinematics. We compare these numbers to a range of $N$-body simulations with different initial conditions. The number of runaways/walkaways is consistent with initial conditions with a high initial stellar density ($\\\\sim$10 000 M$_{\\\\odot}$ pc$^{-3}$), a high initial amount of spatial substructure and either a subvirial or virialised ratio for all subclusters. We also confirm the trajectories of our ejected stars using the data from $Gaia$ EDR3, which reduces the number of runaways from IRS 1/2 from six to four but leaves the number of runaways from S Mon unchanged. The reduction in runaways is due to smaller uncertainties in the proper motion and changes in the parallax/distance estimate for these stars in $Gaia$ EDR3. We find further runaway/walkaway candidates based on proper motion alone in $Gaia$ DR2, which could increase these numbers once radial velocities are available. We also expect further changes in the candidate list with upcoming $Gaia$ data releases.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [astronomical research paper] The study identifies five runaways and nine walkaway stars associated with S Mon, and six runaways and five walkaways linked to IRS 1/2 in the NGC 2264 star-forming region using Gaia DR2 astrometry. These ejected stars' 3D kinematics are used to constrain the initial conditions of the region, favoring high initial stellar density (~10,000 M☉ pc⁻³), significant spatial substructure, and either subvirial or virialised subcluster dynamics. Gaia EDR3 refines the trajectory analysis, reducing IRS 1/2 runaways from six to four due to improved proper motion and distance precision, while maintaining the S Mon results; additional candidates are flagged based on proper motion alone, suggesting future radial velocity data will further refine the population.

source_fields.title: Constraining the initial conditions of NGC 2264 using ejected stars found in Gaia DR2

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: Five of the 3D RW candidates and eleven of the 3D WW candidates traced back to S Mon are confirmed as originating from S Mon after excluding double trace-backs and outliers with flight times exceeding their estimated ages, with the remaining candidates showing velocity and trajectory characteristics inconsistent with typical S Mon initial conditions. Conclusions: Our analysis of ejected RW and WW stars from S Mon, IRS 1, and IRS 2 using Gaia data and $N$-body simulations reveals that all subclusters evolved from initially highly substructured, dense environments with subvirial or virialised velocities, with S Mon and IRS 1/2 sharing similar initial conditions, while the low observed number of WWs suggests limitations in current radial velocity data. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that reconciles the dynamical ejection and binary supernova scenarios by demonstrating how the observed distribution of runaway and walkaway stars can be used to constrain the initial binary fraction and dynamical evolution of star-forming regions, thereby solving the long-standing problem of inferring the original structure and formation history of these regions despite their rapid structural changes. Results from Gaia DR2: The analysis identifies 30 2D-RW and 65 2D-WW candidates traced back to S Mon, with 21 RW candidates and 10 of them further confirmed as 3D-candidates, providing age estimates via PARSEC isochrones and supporting the conclusion that these stars were ejected within the last 5 Myr, with velocity and flight time data enabling detailed kinematic and evolutionary constraints. Gaia DR2 Data analysis: This section describes the methodology for analyzing Gaia DR2 data to identify ejected stars from NGC 2264 subclusters by aligning stellar positions and velocities to the cluster frame, defining ejection candidates based on backward trajectories within a 2 pc radial boundary, and using proper motions and velocity corrections to estimate flight times and ejection histories. Target and data selection: This section identifies NGC 2264 as a spatially extended, young star-forming region with multiple subclusters, variable stellar densities, and age gradients, serving as the target for searching RW and WW stars within a distance range of 720–750 pc with significant uncertainty. Appendix C Past visitors to NGC 2264: This section identifies past visitors to NGC 2264, including stars ejected from S Mon and IRS 1/2, which are older than the main clusters but young enough to have originated from them, providing evidence for dynamical interactions and stellar ejection histories. Appendix D Future visitors to NGC 2264: This appendix identifies and characterizes stars from the Gaia DR2 catalog that can be traced forward in 3D to NGC 2264 within 5–8 million years, providing velocity, radial velocity, age, and spectral type data to constrain the cluster's initial conditions. Appendix E Red giants visiting NGC 2264: This section lists red giant branch stars traced to NGC 2264 with significant velocity uncertainties, which are excluded from the main analysis but noted as potential younger candidates due to their large errors. RW and WW stars from N-body simulations: This section describes how $N$-body simulations are used to model the initial spatial and kinematic substructure of NGC 2264 using fractal distributions and varying virial ratios, to predict the formation and evolution of RW and WW stars. Appendix B 2D-RW and WW candidates from S Mon and IRS 1/2: This appendix presents 2D-RW and WW candidate stars traced back to S Mon and IRS 1/2, using Gaia DR2 data and literature sources, to constrain the initial conditions and ejection histories of NGC 2264. Appendix A RW/WW predictions from simulations for the full mass range: This section presents simulation-based predictions of the number of runaway (RW) and wide-binary (WW) stars expected in young star-forming regions across a range of masses and simulation initial conditions, showing how ejected star populations evolve over time.

abstract: Fast, ejected stars have been found around several young star-forming regions, such as the Orion Nebula Cluster (ONC).
These ejected stars can be used to constrain the initial density, spatial and kinematic substructure when compared to predictions from $N$-body simulations.
We search for runaway and slower walkaway stars using $Gaia$ DR2 within 100 pc of NGC 2264, which contains subclustered regions around higher-mass OB-stars (S Mon, IRS 1 and IRS 2).

We also confirm the trajectories of our ejected stars using the data from $Gaia$ EDR3, which reduces the number of runaways from IRS 1/2 from six to four but leaves the number of runaways from S Mon unchanged
The reduction in runaways is due to smaller uncertainties in the proper motion and changes in the parallax/distance estimate for these stars in $Gaia$ EDR3.
title: Constraining the initial conditions of NGC 2264 using ejected stars found in Gaia DR2

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper identifies specific runaway/walkaway stars from Gaia DR2/EDR3, provides counts (5 runaways, 9 walkaways from S Mon; 4-6 runaways, 5 walkaways from IRS 1/2), and discusses candidate lists refined with improved astrometry.

papers.title: Forming Young and Hypervelocity Stars in the Galactic Centre via Tidal Disruption of a Molecular Cloud
papers.author_names: A. Generozov S. Nayakshin A. M. Madigan

abstract.text: The Milky Way Galaxy hosts a four million solar mass black hole, Sgr A*, that underwent a major accretion episode approximately 3-6 Myr ago. During the episode, hundreds of young massive stars formed in a disc orbiting Sgr A* in the central half parsec. The recent discovery of a hypervelocity star S5-HVS1, ejected by Sgr A* five Myr ago with a velocity vector consistent with the disc, suggests that this event also produced binary star disruptions. The initial stellar disc has to be rather eccentric for this to occur. Such eccentric disks can form from the tidal disruptions of molecular clouds. Here we perform simulations of such disruptions, focusing on gas clouds on rather radial initial orbits. As a result, stars formed in our simulations are on very eccentric orbits ($\\\\bar{e}\\\\sim 0.6$) with a lopsided configuration. For some clouds counter-rotating stars are formed. As in previous work, we find that such discs undergo a secular gravitational instability that leads to a moderate number of particles obtaining eccentricities of 0.99 or greater, sufficient for stellar binary disruption. We also reproduce the mean eccentricity of the young disk in the Galactic centre, though not the observed surface density profile. We discuss missing physics and observational biases that may explain this discrepancy. We conclude that observed S-stars, hypervelocity stars, and disc stars tightly constrain the initial cloud parameters, indicating a cloud mass between a few$\\\\times 10^4$ and $10^5 M_{\\\\odot}$, and a velocity between $\\\\sim 40$ and 80 km s$^{-1}$ at 10 pc.

brief.tldr: [research paper] Hydrodynamical simulations of molecular cloud tidal disruption near Sgr A* reveal that radial infall of clouds $10^4-10^5 M_\odot$ produces lopsided, eccentric gaseous discs with mean stellar eccentricity $\bar{e} \sim 0.6$, enabling secular gravitational instability that excites a subset of stars to $e \gtrsim 0.99$, sufficient for binary disruption via the Hills mechanism. The resulting stellar disc kinematics reproduce observed eccentricities of S-stars and hypervelocity star S5-HVS1, constraining initial cloud mass and velocity to $\sim 40-80$ km s$^{-1}$ at 10 pc, though discrepancies remain in surface density profiles due to missing physics or observational biases.

source_fields.title: Forming Young and Hypervelocity Stars in the Galactic Centre via Tidal Disruption of a Molecular Cloud

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Summary: Tidal disruption of a molecular cloud with velocities of ~40–80 km s⁻¹ and masses of ~10⁴–10⁵ M⊙ can explain the young clockwise disk and S-star cluster in the Galactic center, while predicting higher eccentricities for non-disc stars and a unimodal eccentricity distribution in observed disk stars. Discussion: The inclusion of unresolved binaries and realistic stellar mass functions increases the observed disc eccentricity and introduces unbound stars, while a flatter stellar density profile and the absence of massive stars or compact objects reduce the tension with observations, suggesting that the actual eccentricity and surface density of the Galactic center stellar disk are likely lower than in simplified models. Introduction: This paper proposes a novel mechanism in which a lopsided, eccentric disk formed from turbulent molecular cloud infall or collisions excites binaries to radial orbits via precession, leading to tidal disruption and the formation of the S-star cluster from disc-born or background binaries, thereby solving the long-standing problem of the origin and kinematic connection between the S-stars and the clockwise disk. $N$-body simulations: This section describes the methodology for conducting high-resolution $N$-body simulations of stellar discs around the SMBH, focusing on binary disruptions and the formation of S-stars by initializing simulations from hydrodynamic snapshots, using adaptive timesteps and excluding gas and binaries to efficiently model the dominant stellar cusp dynamics. Hydrodynamical simulations: This section describes a hydrodynamical simulation framework using Gadget-2 to model gas dynamics, star formation, and SMBH accretion in the Galactic center, incorporating a realistic stellar cusp potential and self-consistent radiative cooling. Joint gas dynamics and stellar N-body simulations: This section presents a two-step numerical approach—first SPH simulations of gas dynamics and disc star formation, followed by N-body simulations of stellar orbital evolution—to model star formation and the potential formation of S-stars near Sgr A* while overcoming numerical limitations of a unified simulation.

title: Forming Young and Hypervelocity Stars in the Galactic Centre via Tidal Disruption of a Molecular Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper on tidal disruption of molecular clouds to form eccentric stellar discs and hypervelocity stars via the Hills mechanism. No new observational stellar data presented.

papers.title: Probing the shape of the Milky Way dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars: a new method
papers.author_names: Arianna Gallo Luisa Ostorero Sankha Subhra Chakrabarty Stefano Ebagezio Antonaldo Diaferio

abstract.text: We propose a new method to determine the shape of the gravitational potential of the dark matter (DM) halo of the Milky Way (MW) with the galactocentric tangential velocities of a sample of hypervelocity stars (HVSs). We compute the trajectories of different samples of HVSs in a MW where the baryon distribution is axisymmetric and the DM potential either is spherical or is spheroidal or triaxial with radial-dependent axis ratios. We determine the shape of the DM potential with the distribution of the latitudinal velocity $|v_{\\\\vartheta}|$ in axisymmetric Galactic potentials, or with the distribution of $|v_{\\\\vartheta}|$ and of a function $\\\\bar v_{\\\\varphi}$ of the azimuthal velocity in non-axisymmetric Galactic potentials. We recover the correct shape of the DM potential by comparing the distribution of $|v_{\\\\vartheta}|$ and $\\\\bar v_{\\\\varphi}$ against the corresponding distributions of mock samples of HVSs that traveled in DM halos of different shapes. We use the largest possible sample of $\\\\sim 800$ HVSs of $4~M_\\\\odot$ ejected with the Hills mechanism at a rate $\\\\sim 10^{-4}$ yr$^{-1}$, currently outgoing, and located at more than 10 kpc from the Galactic center. In our ideal case of galactocentric velocities with null uncertainties and no observational limitations, our method recovers the correct shape of the DM potential with a success rate $S\\\\gtrsim 89\\\\%$ in axisymmetric Galactic potentials, and $S > 96\\\\%$ in the explored non-axisymmetric cases. The unsuccessful cases yield axis ratios of the DM potential that are off by $\\\\pm 0.1$. The success rate decreases with decreasing sample size: for example, for a spherical DM halo, $S$ drops from $\\\\sim 98\\\\%$ to $\\\\sim 38\\\\%$ when the sample size decreases from $\\\\sim 800$ to $\\\\sim 40$ HVSs. A robust determination of the shape of the DM potential thus requires the measure of the galactocentric velocity of a few hundred genuine HVSs.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A novel kinematic method uses the galactocentric tangential velocities of hypervelocity stars (HVSs) to infer the shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo. By simulating HVS trajectories in axisymmetric or non-axisymmetric DM potentials—spherical, spheroidal, or triaxial—the method compares observed distributions of $|v_{\vartheta}|$ and a derived $\bar{v}_{\varphi}$ function to mock samples, achieving >96% success in recovering the true halo shape under ideal conditions with ~800 HVSs; success drops sharply below 40 stars, and deviations exceed ±0.1 in axis ratios when recovery fails. The approach highlights the need for robust velocity measurements of hundreds of HVSs to constrain halo triaxiality beyond current observational limits.

source_fields.title: Probing the shape of the Milky Way dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars: a new method

source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: Hypervelocity stars serve as a powerful probe of the Milky Way's dark matter halo triaxiality over large spatial scales, with simulations showing their phase space distributions recover axis ratios accurately despite limitations in sample size and mass assumptions, particularly when ejected with velocities above 730 km s⁻¹ via mechanisms like the Hills process. Conclusions: This study presents a robust method to infer the shape of the Milky Way's dark matter halo from the distribution of galactocentric tangential velocities of hypervelocity stars, achieving success rates above 89% in axisymmetric and over 96% in non-axisymmetric cases using statistical tests on velocity indicators. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that reconciles discrepancies in DM halo shape constraints by incorporating baryonic physics and multi-tracer observations in a consistent, self-consistent model, addressing the problem of inconsistent halo shape predictions across different observational scales and methods. Sample size and method success rate: The paper proposes a statistical method that uses the median $p$-values from Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests to assess the shape of the dark matter halo by comparing observed hypervelocity star samples with mock samples in different halo shapes, demonstrating that the success rate of recovering the correct halo shape increases with sample size. Indicators of the shape of the DM halo: This section identifies phase space coordinates of hypervelocity stars that are most sensitive to the triaxiality of the dark matter halo by comparing distributions across mock catalogs with different halo shapes using a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test. Numerical simulations and mock catalogs: This section describes the numerical simulation of hypervelocity star ejection velocities from the Galactic Center using a 3-body model, incorporating realistic binary and orbital parameters to generate a velocity distribution that serves as the foundation for subsequent mock catalog construction. Appendix A Mock catalogs and observed sample: This section presents the kinematic properties of hypervelocity stars in a mock catalog with a triaxial dark matter halo, showing how selection criteria mimic observed samples and highlight the influence of halo shape on stellar velocity distributions. A new method to constrain the shape of the DM halo: The paper proposes a method that uses the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test to compare the distribution of shape indicators from a real sample of hypervelocity stars with mock distributions generated from DM halos of varying shapes, identifying the halo shape that best matches the observed data by selecting the one yielding the highest p-value under the null hypothesis of identical distributions. Appendix B Transformation of coordinates and velocities: This section details the mathematical transformations of stellar positions and velocities from the Galactic heliocentric system to the equatorial coordinate system, including the conversion of coordinates and proper motions using standard astronomical formulas and reference values. Constraining the shape of the DM halo in an axisymmetric Galactic potential: This section demonstrates that the method can effectively recover the axial ratio $q_z$ of the dark matter halo from the distribution of the latitudinal velocity magnitude $|v_\\vartheta|$ of hypervelocity stars in an axisymmetric Galactic potential. Constraining the shape of the DM halo in a non-axisymmetric Galactic potential: This section demonstrates that the distributions of tangential velocity components $|v_{\\vartheta}|$ and $\\bar{v}_{\\varphi}$ in hypervelocity stars can effectively constrain the triaxial shape of a non-axisymmetric dark matter halo by recovering the axial ratios $q_y$ and $q_z$.

abstract: We propose a new method to determine the shape of the gravitational potential of the dark matter (DM) halo of the Milky Way (MW) with the galactocentric tangential velocities of a sample of hypervelocity
title: Probing the shape of the Milky Way dark matter halo with hypervelocity stars: a new method

catalog_assessment.evidence: Methodology paper proposing a new kinematic technique to constrain dark matter halo shape using simulated hypervelocity star samples. Uses mock samples, not real observational stellar data.

papers.title: MOCCA-SURVEY Database II -- Properties of Intermediate Mass Black Holes escaping from star clusters
papers.author_names: Konrad Maliszewski Mirek Giersz Dorota Gondek-Rosińska Abbas Askar Arkadiusz Hypki

abstract.text: In this work we investigate properties of intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) that escape from star clusters due to dynamical interactions. The studied models were simulated as part of the preliminary second survey carried out using the MOCCA code (MOCCA-SURVEY Database II), which is based on the Monte Carlo N-body method and does not include gravitational wave recoil kick prescriptions of the binary black hole merger product. We have found that IMBHs are more likely to be formed and ejected in models where both initial central density and central escape velocities have high values. Most of our studied objects escape in a binary with another black hole (BH) as their companion and have masses between $100$ and $140\\\\: M_{\\\\odot}$. Escaping IMBHs tend to build-up mass most effectively through repeated mergers in a binary with BHs due to gravitational wave emission. Binaries play a key role in their ejection from the system as they allow these massive objects to gather energy needed for escape. The binaries in which IMBHs escape tend to have very high binding energy at the time of escape and the last interaction is strong but does not involve a massive intruder. These IMBHs gain energy needed to escape the cluster gradually in successive dynamical interactions. We present specific examples of the history of IMBH formation and escape from star cluster models. We also discuss the observational implications of our findings as well as the potential influence of the gravitational wave recoil kicks on the process.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [survey / numerical simulation study] Numerical N-body simulations of star clusters reveal that intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) with masses 100–140 M⊙ are more likely to form and escape when initial cluster central density and escape velocity are high. Escaping IMBHs typically form binaries with other black holes, where repeated gravitational wave-driven mergers enable mass buildup and energy accumulation through sequential dynamical interactions. These binaries exhibit high binding energy at ejection, with escape driven by gradual energy gain rather than a single violent event, suggesting that binary dynamics are essential for IMBH ejection and growth in dense stellar environments.

source_fields.title: MOCCA-SURVEY Database II -- Properties of Intermediate Mass Black Holes escaping from star clusters

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Models: The paper proposes using the MOCCA code to simulate the dynamical and stellar evolution of globular clusters with multiple stellar populations, focusing on testing dynamical mixing in very dense models and incorporating realistic supernova and binary evolution physics to reproduce observed cluster properties. Discussion: IMBHs are most likely ejected from dense star clusters with initial central densities of $ \\sim 3 \\times 10^6 - 10^7\\, \\mathrm{M}_{\\odot}\\,\\mathrm{pc}^{-3} $, where 68 escapers (mostly below $ 200\\, \\mathrm{M}_{\\odot} $) are observed, with a dynamical ejection probability of $ 10\\% - 22\\% $, though results are upper limits due to neglected gravitational recoil kicks and lower densities in real clusters reduce IMBH formation and ejection rates. Introduction: This paper presents a novel framework for IMBH formation in galactic nuclei via dynamical runaway collisions in low-metallicity star clusters, resolving the long-standing problem of how massive black holes in the 10²–10⁵ M⊙ range can form despite the pair-instability supernova mass gap that suppresses isolated stellar evolution pathways. Cause Of The Escape: IMBHs escape globular clusters primarily due to a single strong final interaction—often a close fly-by or exchange—where the binding energy change exceeds the tidal energy, driven not by the intruder's mass but by its extremely close approach to a hard IMBH binary. Escapers Parameters: This section shows that most IMBH escape events occur in binaries, with mass ratios peaking near 0.1 and 0.3–0.4, eccentricities consistent with thermal equilibrium after multiple interactions, and escaped IMBH masses typically below 300 M⊙, with one exceptional case reaching ~600 M⊙. Gravitational Kicks: This section quantifies the retention probability of black hole binary merger products in globular clusters using gravitational kick prescriptions from Morawski et al. (2018), finding that mergers with similar masses and low spins have higher retention probabilities, especially in the first merger event. Imbh Escape Examples: This section demonstrates that intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs) can escape globular clusters either individually, through dynamical interactions that increase their orbital eccentricity and binding energy, or as binaries, such as in a case where a flyby of a massive intruder provides the necessary energy for ejection. Mass Build-Up Events: Escaping intermediate-mass black holes grow primarily through repeated BBH mergers via gravitational wave emission, while those remaining in the cluster acquire most of their mass through collisions, particularly with other black holes. Initial Model Parameters: The paper proposes a systematic analysis of 212 star cluster models from the MOCCA-SURVEY Database II to investigate the dependence of intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) formation and ejection on initial cluster parameters, finding that higher initial central density and escape velocity promote IMBH formation and a peak in ejection probability at intermediate densities due to enhanced dynamical interactions.

abstract: Escaping IMBHs tend to build-up mass most effectively through repeated mergers in a binary with BHs due to gravitational wave emission.
title: MOCCA-SURVEY Database II -- Properties of Intermediate Mass Black Holes escaping from star clusters

catalog_assessment.evidence: Numerical simulation study from MOCCA-SURVEY Database II investigating IMBH formation and ejection dynamics in star clusters. No observational stellar catalog or object-level data presented.
